prompt: A LEED BD+C office building project team is implementing demand-controlled ventilation (DCV) using CO2 sensors to modulate outdoor air intake based on actual occupancy. The system is designed to maintain CO2 levels below 1,000 ppm while reducing energy consumption during periods of low occupancy. What is the most important factor for the commissioning agent to verify to ensure the DCV system achieves its intended energy savings?

options.text: CO2 sensor calibration and response time under varying occupancy conditions
Integration with the fire alarm system for emergency ventilation override
Compliance with minimum outdoor air requirements per ASHRAE 62.1 at all operating conditions
Coordination with operable windows to prevent conflicts with natural ventilation

explanation: Energy savings from DCV depend on sensors accurately detecting occupancy-related CO2 changes and the ventilation system responding quickly enough to reduce outdoor air during low-occupancy periods. If sensors drift or response is sluggish, the system will over-ventilate and lose the intended savings even when minimum code ventilation is maintained.

distractorExplanations.b: Fire alarm integration is important for life safety, but it is not the primary determinant of DCV energy performance.
distractorExplanations.c: Maintaining ASHRAE 62.1 minimum outdoor air is required for health and compliance, yet it does not by itself confirm that the system modulates airflow efficiently to save energy.
distractorExplanations.d: Operable window coordination may matter on mixed-mode projects, but it is not the central commissioning check for DCV energy savings in this scenario.
